entry.title: SAP ALM Experience Day -Masterclass Edition! : Bellevue, WA
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

We’re excited to invite you to our in-person ALM Experience Day – Masterclass Edition, taking place in Bellevue, Washington, on March 24, 2026.

This full‑day event is designed to provide valuable insights and hands‑on experience in managing operations and implementations with SAP Cloud ALM, helping you accelerate business transformation and operational efficiency.

Why attend?

Gain firsthand exposure to the latest advancements in SAP Cloud ALM for both implementation and operations. You will have the opportunity to earn the Solution Transformation Practitioner Badge by completing the assessment, while connecting with SAP experts and peers to exchange insights, trends, and best practices.

Event Highlights:

  • Understand the importance of a robust ALM strategy to drive business growth and agility.
  • Gain in‑depth knowledge of SAP Cloud ALM and its role in supporting intelligent cloud and hybrid landscapes.
  • Participate in hands-on sessions for implementation and operations
  • Engage in interactive sessions with SAP ALM experts and network with industry peers.
  • Discover how to seamlessly transition to SAP Cloud ALM, the go-to platform for all your Application Lifecycle Management needs as an SAP customer.
  • Enhance your expertise by completing the Solution Transformation Practitioner Badge test, demonstrating your ability to manage the design‑to‑deploy phase of business transformation using an Integrated Toolchain.

Event Details:

  • Date: March 24th, 2026
  • Venue: 601 108th Ave NE, Suite 1000, Bellevue, WA 98004, USA
  • Time: 10:00 a.m. - 4:00 p.m. (PST)

Registration is free, but subject to approval!

Register now : https://events.sap.com/sap-alm-events-na/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Re: SAP Generative AI Hub: Using the Python SDK for SAP Data Tasks (Part 2 of 6)
entry.content: SAP AI Launchpad is a separate BTP application (subscribed alongside AI Core) that provides a web-based UI for the Generative AI Hub. Within AI Launchpad, there is a built-in Jupyter-style notebook environment where you can write and run Python code directly in the browser — no local Python installation needed. The key advantage is that your AI Core credentials are pre-injected into the notebook environment automatically, so you skip the ".aicore-config.json" setup entirely. It's the fastest way to run your first Python SDK call without any local configuration. If you're already using AI Launchpad to access the prompt editor, look for the Notebooks section in the left navigation.

entry.title: How to create WTs in EWM without a batch?
entry.content:
Hello,

We work with a Storage Type that stores multiple batches in the same storage bin.

When creating the picking WTs, system determines a specific batch to be created.
However, in this multi batch ST, warehouse workers would take the lot that is closest to them, to avoid downtime from having to move all the pallets to look for a particular lot that is filled in the WT.

So, how can we create picking WTs without a predetermined batch?

If you can make a recommendation to improve this in operation, it is also welcome.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to create WTs in EWM without a batch?
entry.content:
Hello,
What you describe can be achieved by changing the given Storage Type customizing in regards to Level of Avail. Qty and Avail. Qty: Batches.
Set it to '1' (storage bin level) and '1' (batch-neutral), respectively.
This way the available stock entries in /SCWM/AQUA will be stored altogether as one stock line only with the full quantity of a given product.
For example, physical stocks of product XYZ have 10 PC of Batch A, 10 PC of Batch B and 10 PC of batch C, in three separate HUs, named 1, 2 and 3.
With Level of Available quantity set to blank (HU) and Avail. Qty: Batches also blank (Batch-specific), your available stocks would be exactly the same as the physical stock entries:
10 PC of product XYZ batch A in HU 1
10 PC of product XYZ batch B in HU 2
10 PC of product XYZ batch C in HU 3
However, with the changes mentioned above the available stocks are stored as: 30 PC of product XYZ. No batch information. No HU information.
You will fill in which HU and which batch is being picked upon WT confirmation in /SCWM/TO_CONF or RFUI.
Doing so you ensure that a source HU and a batch are not predefined in the WT, assuming that:
The outbound delivery does not contain a batch selection criteria.
That no stock lines on the bin have Quality Inspection (QI) reference.
As outlined on KBA 2429679 - Product WT includes source HU despite Storage Type customizing.
Please also refer to restrictions mentioned on KBAs:
2608906 - WT created with batch for Batch-Neutral Storage Type
2463481 - Batch Neutral setting does not work during WT creation
Before making any changes to your Storage Type customizing, please refer to KBA 3483206 - Changes in the Storage Type customizing in SPRO
Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Can all approvers be displayed correctly from the beginning when using BAdI MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2
entry.content:
I am using Workflow for Purchase Requisition with BAdI MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2 to determine approvers dynamically.

The workflow has 4 approval levels.

Example approvers:

  • Level 1 → User A

  • Level 2 → User B

  • Level 3 → User C

  • Level 4 → User D

The issue I see is that when the workflow starts (before anyone approves), the approvers are not displayed correctly for all levels.

For example, at the beginning:

  • Level 1 → correct (User A)

  • Level 2 → correct (User B)

  • Level 3 → shows an incorrect approver (sometimes same as level 1)

  • Level 4 → also incorrect

Then once the workflow progresses:

  • after level 2 is approved → level 3 becomes correct

  • after level 3 is approved → level 4 becomes correct

From tracing the BAdI, I can see that initially it is only called for:

 

 
STEPINFO-currentstep = 001
STEPINFO-currentstep = 002
and it is not called for steps 003 or 004 until the workflow actually reaches those steps.

So my question is:

Is it possible to display the correct approvers for all approval levels from the beginning, before any approval step is completed, when using BAdI MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2?

entry.title: FUE License Consumption Ruleset
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am currently reviewing the SAP licensing model based on FUEs and I’ve run into an issue regarding the ruleset used to calculate FUE consumption.

According to SAP Note 3113382, the FUE measurement relies on a ruleset provided by SAP. However, the ruleset available today appears to be incomplete or outdated, as several usage scenarios and mappings seem to be missing. I haven’t found any official update or a more detailed version of the ruleset.

Does anyone have a more recent ruleset for FUE calculation or any additional documentation / information? 

Any insights, references, or experiences would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: API Management - GET mTLS Serial Number
entry.content:
Hi @Pratola_Gianni ,

You have found the perfect knowledge source for your scenario, as it is mentioned in the SAP Blog: How to Read Client Certificate Details in an API Proxy in SAP API Management first you need to set the 2 mentioned properties:
- <ConnectionProperties>
- <ClientProperties>
to 'true'.
This action can be done only by Operations Team of API Management. Please create a support case for the component OPU-API-OD-OPS with that request and please also mention the URL of your API Portal where you configured that virtual host with mTLS.

Once it is completed you are allowed to read the serial number of the client certificate via 'tls.client.cert.serial' variable.

Thank you,
David

entry.title: Re: ASP.NET Crystal Report Viewer failing to display a re...
entry.content:
Without a support contract you can't submit an Incident, you would have to purchase CR Server which comes with a 1 year contract, last I heard...

So you are using JS/HTML tags in your data?

Can you use dummy data and send me a direct message with a link to download the report with saved data for testing and possibly sending to R&D to look at?

A quick search I found this description of what is happening:

The expression
<A>C</A> in JavaScript is interpreted as a string literal because it is enclosed in double quotes, resulting in a string value that includes the HTML-like tags. 
When the JavaScript interpreter encounters " <A>C</A>", it treats the enclosed characters literally as a sequence of characters within a string. This string value itself does not have any special meaning or function within the core JavaScript language, other than representing the text itself. 
  • String representation: The expression evaluates to the exact string " <A>C</A>".
  • No HTML interpretation (in JavaScript): JavaScript is a scripting language, while HTML is a markup language. The string is not automatically parsed or rendered as HTML in a standard JavaScript environment (e.g., a console or backend environment like Node.js).
  • Potential use in the DOM: This string is most commonly used when interacting with the Document Object Model (DOM) to represent an HTML element. For example, it could be used to set the innerHTML property of an existing element:
    javascript
    // Example usage in a web browser environmentdocument.getElementById('myElement').innerHTML = " <A>C</A>";
    In this context, the browser's rendering engine would interpret the string as HTML, creating an anchor element (<a>) with the content "C" inside it. 
The value itself is a simple string, and its behavior or appearance depends entirely on how the JavaScript code uses it. 
 

I can ask R&D to have look at this post but I doubt they will do anything to resolve your issue.

I suggest not using that format in your data.

Don

PS- The reason I say R&D likely won't do anything is somehting like this has the potential to cause 1000's of existing reports not to work. CR uses js files to manage a lot of data particularly Parameters now use HTML5 to process them.

entry.title: Re: Joule – Getting Started with Document Grounding - setup guide - 1
entry.content:
Hello @MarcoDebattisti ,

I faced a similar issue earlier, which was caused by an expired client certificate. After generating a new certificate and placing it in the folder specified in the Bruno Collection configuration, the issue was resolved.

This problem is likely due to the client certificate being expired or not configured correctly in Bruno → Collection Settings → Client Certificates. Please verify that the client certificate is valid and that it has been placed in the correct folder path as mentioned in the Bruno Collection settings.

Thanks,
Indrajeet

entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
Day 6, an evening session

We need to talk about Codex.

Because wow, this thing can plan!

Wise people have been saying, switch the Model into a Plan mode first. Ask it to analyse the codebase, make a plan. Revise, then execute.

Naturally, I paid them no heed. I would always jump directly into execution — with mixed results, reworks, and manual fixes.

Not today. Today I put Codex into Plan mode. Why today? Mostly just to try something new. Why Codex? Because I hear he is especially adept at planning and architecting. And man, can he draw up a plan!

I decided to stay with Fiori web apps for now, because I’d like to get to a state where I can specify one and AI will create it end-to-end. It may still take a while, if I manage to get there at all. 

For today I chose NorthWind Traders: Extended version. The simplified version didn’t quite work out, then it stands to reason that the more complicated one will, right? A grandes problemas grandes remedios, as they say in Spain.

In lieu of Specification I directed Codex to the Database Schema at GitHub. After 1 minute and 26 seconds he came back with a summary approach, a 16-point plan, and 6 recommended delivery phases.

Honestly, I was blown away by this. It’s too much text to paste here, here is a link to a Gist. It contains the entirety of today’s conversation, the Plan I am referring to is lines 41-133.

Then I continued instructing my new best friend Codex to construct the RAP artifacts - or rather just kept responding “Yes please” to his suggestions, correcting the course only slightly from time to time. I won’t bore you with details (it’s all in the Gist), just wanted to point out a few observations:

  • Codex was able to detect that this Database Table was denormalised:

At first he created the table as defined in the defective Schema, but kept reminding me of this fact throughout the conversation until we fixed it. This impressed me much. So he’s got the brains and he’s got the touch?

  • He made a mistake of creating all Currency Amounts with 4 decimals. No-one complained until the Service Binding. Another obvious candidate for inclusion into a Skill! 🙄
  • I love that Codex CLI shows you the percentage of remaining context at all times. Comes in super-handy to know when to /compact.

  • Another thing I like is that it shows you the consumed tokens at the end of the conversation. Gives you an idea.

Just as a curiosity, this would have cost me about $16 if I had to pay for tokens. And it wasn't even the entire conversation.

 

 

From Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering

Both these terms were coined by Andrej Karpathy about a year apart, and I now find myself in the need of transitioning between the two. Right now I’m a vibe coder. I do everything in just one context window (which gets compacted from time to time - either automatically or be me), using the most capable model, burning through tokens like there is no tomorrow. This is matar moscas a cañonazos, to stay with Spanish sayings - killing flies with cannonshots. I make no distinction between explorative tasks (“Read the contents of the SAP Package ZNORTH”), for which sub-Agents could be spawned using cheaper quicker Models like Haiku or GPT-5-mini, and thinking tasks where more capable Models are required. I have a vague idea how to orchestrate such a setup (define custom subagents in the project’s .claude/agents with a focused role (e.g. codebase-explorer, log-analyzer), then explicitly ask Claude to “delegate X to the Y agent”), but I am nowhere near that level yet. Hell, I don’t even know how to create and use Skills properly.

As a result, the Antigravity Agent has given up the ghost, said to himself probably This guy is an idiot.

I could throw money at it, but let’s try to do things right first. Agentic Engineering.

 

Anyways. Baby steps. I only installed Claude Code and Codex 6 days ago, after all 😅

entry.title: SAP Developer News, March 12th, 2026
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

SAP Intelligence Event

  • Event Details: https://taiefy.com/
  • SAP CodeJams: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/gh-p/code-jam

HANA-CLI 4.0

  • NPM: https://www.npmjs.com/package/hana-cli
  • GitHub Repo: https://github.com/SAP-samples/hana-developer-cli-tool-example
  • Documentation Site: https://sap-samples.github.io/hana-developer-cli-tool-example/
  • Blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-blogs-posts/major-new-version-of-the-hana-cli-how-it-was-built-with-the-help-of-ai-and/ba-p/14346752

 SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable #2

  • Customer & Partner Roundtable for SAP BTP Platform Automation #2 (March Edition) https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-amp-partner-roundtable-for-sap-btp-platform-automation-2-march/ba-p/14339369

SAP Fiori Development Newsletter

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-fiori-development-newsletter-issue-39-march-2026/ba-p/14334703

SAP CodeJam at Bosch-Vietnam

  • SAP CodeJams: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/gh-p/code-jam

 SAP Developer Insights Survey

  • The 2026 SAP Developer Insights Survey is live: https://sapinsights.eu.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_9prRAJk5ccYPMMe
  • 2025 Survey Report: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/the-2025-developer-insights-survey-the-report/ba-p/14323198

DYK #7 AI Launchpad Model Library

  • More Info: https://help.sap.com/docs/ai-launchpad/sap-ai-launchpad/model-library

=============================================================

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro

0:10 SAP Intelligence Event

1:26 hana-cli 4.0

2:48 SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable #2

3:45 SAP Fiori Development Newsletter

4:36 SAP CodeJam at Bosch-Vietnam

5:50 SAP Developer Insights Survey 

6:35 DYK #7 AI Launchpad Model Library

 Transcription

00;00;00;02 - 00;00;05;01

This is the SAP Developer News for March 12th, 2026.

00;00;05;04 - 00;00;09;26

Hey, everybody, we're here at the SAP Intelligence Event and we're on the final day. We just completed

00;00;09;29 - 00;00;25;18

all the CodeJams and I'll say this event was amazing. We had great collaborations, very good conversations. The hosts very friendly. So I can recommend that to come here next year. It's going to happen and Nora want to say something

00;00;25;25 - 00;00;43;08

Yeah actually we did three different CodeJams today. So one was using Joule Studio and building agents with Joule studio and the other one was getting started with Gen AI Hub. And the one that I love most is our new CodeJam was, which is the, I got did we call it: Building Code Based Agents on BTP

00;00;43;10 - 00;01;05;06

Anyway, it's about how you can build your own AI agents and deploy them to BTP using Gen AI Hub LiteLLM and CrueAI and we actually solve the little murder mystery, so that was a lot of fun. Oh, yeah. That was the long one. Yeah. And I was here just to take selfies, and I was here just to administrate things like, you know, create the users or an email address.

00;01;05;06 - 00;01;23;07

Now, has it been a great time? Really good to hang out with the colleagues again and, meet all my friends here in Amsterdam, if you're interested in a CodeJam in your area or you going to do is request, and we'll put a link to the, so that we'll put a link to that in the show notes.

00;01;23;10 - 00;01;26;13

Bye for now.

00;01;26;16 - 00;02;00;29

Earlier this week, I released a brand new 4.0 version of the Hana command line interface. Now there's tons of new functionality in here. From everything from a brand new documentation site to, an MCP server and lots of new commands that have been implemented, performance improvements, all kinds of stuff. But how it is able to do all this new functionality in a relatively short amount of time has to do with the usage of AI coding assistance.

00;02;01;02 - 00;02;26;23

And I've recently written a little blog post that details my experience with these code assistants. What went well? What didn't go so well, and some suggestions on how to optimize usage of such code assistance. So if you're a user of Hana command line interface, which is a tool for any developers out there that use Hana or the cloud application programing model, I suggest that you check out the brand new version.

00;02;26;26 - 00;02;49;18

Or if you're just interested in what it's like to take a major project and use code assistance heavily on them, then you might, check out the blog post, even if you're not a Hana CLI user. So links to all the things I mentioned are available in the video description.

entry.title: Re: Enlarging the use of BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST
entry.content:
Thanks. A few remarks.

Only the parameter C_ACCIT has lines containing the components BAPI_PARAM and BAPI_TABIX, so your code READ TABLE <fs_table> WITH KEY ('BAPI_PARAM') = ... would fail (ITAB_ILLEGAL_COMPONENT) if trying to change C_ACCCR or other parameters of the method IF_EX_ACC_DOCUMENT~CHANGE of the BAdI ACC_DOCUMENT. Suggestion: it's not worth programming dynamically, it obfuscates the logic for no good reason.

The usual logic behind the EXTENSION parameter of any BAPI is to fill STRUCTURE with the name of an existing DDIC structure (usually created by the customer or the partner) and VALUEPART1 to VALUEPART4 contain the values of components of the DDIC structure (can be mapped via the methods CL_ABAP_CONTAINER_UTILITIES=>FILL_CONTAINER_C and READ_CONTAINER_C).

One screenshot is missing which can help understanding better the code, it's the components BAPI_PARAM and BAPI_TABIX of the parameter C_ACCIT of the method IF_EX_ACC_DOCUMENT~CHANGE of the BAdI ACC_DOCUMENT . NB: the screenshot is showing my own data (passed to BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST).

As a reminder, here's your code, where <fs_table> is in fact C_ACCIT:

          READ TABLE <fs_table> WITH KEY
                                  ('BAPI_PARAM') = <fs_ext>-structure
                                  ('BAPI_TABIX') = <fs_ext>-valuepart1
                                ASSIGNING <fs_header>.

The parameter EXTENSION2 of BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST needs to contain these values to change C_ACCIT-KOSTL and C_ACCHD-GLVOR (VALUEPART4 contains the values):

entry.title: Re: UNDERSTAND TAX PROCEDURE ASSIGNMENT
entry.content:
Dear @cirill0d,

Question 1.

No, not as the primary standard driver. SAP says the field used in Finance to determine the correct tax procedure is Tax Departure Country/Region, not simply the sales order company code country

Question 2.

Yes. SAP explicitly states that Tax Departure Country/Region is by default derived from the country/region of the plant from which the goods are delivered. Therefore in the normal standard sales process the determining country is effectively the plant country

Question 3.

No, not directly. The country of the company code assigned to the plant is not the main determination field. It becomes relevant mainly as a restriction in cases where RITA is not active. In that case, SAP states that foreign plants cannot be used to alter Tax Departure Country/Region and only a country/region equal to the company code country can be set in the sales order header

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3308072

Summary:

In Public Cloud the tax procedure is determined via Tax Departure Country/Region. In standard sales processing, this field is derived from the delivering plant, so the determining country is effectively the plant country. It is not primarily taken from the sales order company code country nor from the country of the company code assigned to the plant

Best Regards,

Dawid

Currently, it's not possible to set up a VLS class without configuring a VLS Not-for-credit Completion Status. As seen in your screenshot, this is a mandatory field and there is no setting / configuration to change this behavior.

Best regards,

entry.title: " &prompt=none" response back to customer's IDP
entry.content:
We are implementing IAS for a customer and the customer is requiring that the parameter "prompt=none" be included in the OIDC authorization request to their IdP.

Prompt is typically an optional parameter. Without it, the client system should detect whether the user already has a valid login session and only prompt for authentication if one does not exist. Which is the most secure option and in my experience IAS is always configured with the strictest options.

IAS does not provide a way to alter or append parameters in the OIDC request it sends to the client IdP.

Thoughts on how to pass the '&prompt=none' string back to customer's idp?

entry.title: Campaigns in SmartRecruiters Talent Communities: A Complete Guide to Candidate Engagement
entry.content:
Recruitment today goes beyond simply posting jobs and reviewing applications. Organizations are increasingly investing in candidate engagement strategies to build long-term relationships with potential talent. One of the most powerful tools that supports this strategy in SmartRecruiters Talent Communities (SmartCRM) is the Campaigns feature.

Campaigns allow recruiters and talent acquisition teams to engage, nurture, and communicate with talent pools through targeted communication such as emails, newsletters, event invitations, and job alerts. By leveraging campaigns, organizations can maintain strong relationships with passive candidates and convert them into applicants when the right opportunities arise.

This blog explains Campaigns in SmartRecruiters Talent Communities, including their overview, features, benefits, use cases, and step-by-step configuration process.

What are Campaigns in SmartRecruiters Talent Communities?

A Campaign in SmartRecruiters is a recruitment marketing activity used to communicate with candidates who are part of Talent Communities or candidate pools.

Campaigns enable recruiters to:
• Send targeted emails to specific candidate communities
• Promote upcoming hiring events
• Share employer branding content
• Notify candidates about relevant job opportunities
• Nurture passive candidates over time

These campaigns are part of the SmartRecruiters CRM capability, designed to help organizations build a proactive talent pipeline rather than relying only on job applications.

Key Features of Campaigns in SmartRecruiters

1. Targeted Candidate Communication
Campaigns allow recruiters to send communications to specific talent communities or filtered candidate groups, ensuring that messages are relevant to the recipients.

2. Email Campaigns
Recruiters can create professional email campaigns that include:
• Job alerts
• Company updates
• Hiring event invitations
• Talent newsletters
Emails can be customized with branding elements such as company logos, images, and call-to-action buttons.

3. Candidate Segmentation
Campaigns can target candidates based on:
• Talent community membership
• Skills and job preferences
• Location
• Tags or attributes
• Previous candidate activity

4. Event Promotion
Campaigns can be used to promote:
• Virtual hiring events
• Campus drives
• Career fairs
• Webinar sessions

5. Automation and Scheduling
Recruiters can schedule campaigns to run at specific times or dates.
Examples:
• Monthly talent newsletter
• Reminder emails before hiring events
• Follow-up campaigns after events

6. Campaign Performance Analytics
SmartRecruiters provides campaign insights such as:
• Email open rates
• Click-through rates
• Candidate engagement
• Conversion to applications

These metrics help recruiters measure the effectiveness of candidate engagement strategies

Before you start creating Campaigns, you need to create a Talent Community.

Configuration Steps for Talent Communities in SmartRecruiters

Step 1: Create Talent Communities

  1. Navigate to the Communities tab.
    2. Click Create Community.
    3. Enter the following information:
    • Community Name
    • Description
    • Job Function / Skill Category
    • Location (optional)
    • Tags or keywords
    4. Define community ownership (recruiter or team).
    5. Save the community.

Step 2: Configure Community Screening Questions

Screening questions help automatically categorize candidates into the right communities.

  1. Navigate to Settings → Screening Questions.
  2. Create a Question Set.
  3. Define questions such as:
    • What is your primary skill?
    • What location are you interested in?
    • What type of roles interest you?
  4. Link the question set to a community lead capture form.

Step 3: Create Lead Capture Forms

  1. Create Signup Form – Go to the “Form” tab of the Talent Community and click on the “Create form” button.

 

  1. Enter all required information in the form, such as Name, Brand, Logo, Description, resume, fields to be filled by the candidate, etc., and click on the “Publish form” button.

 

Admin can also click on the “Preview” button to see the preview of the form.

Configuration Steps for Campaign in SmartRecruiters

1- Create Campaign               

  1. Click on any existing Talent Community and go to the “Campaign” tab, and click on the “Create campaign” button to start creating your campaign.

  1. Define the “Audience entry rules”, “Audience exit rules”, Timeframe (Start and End Date) of the campaign, Language, and a message you want to trigger after launching the campaign, and click on the Live/Update button.

  1. To design the message, click on the “Content” button under the Message section.

  1. Once you click on the “Content” button, the system will show you a page where you can design your campaign email notification.

You can use Text, Image, Button, and a divider to design your email notification under the “Blocks” section.

By clicking on the “Apply Now” button, you can also set up the hyperlink to your career site page.

You can define the background color under the “Styles” section.

You can also save your template for future reference under the “Templates” Section

You can also define your email campaign footer under the “Footer” section.

  1. You can also define your email Message subject, Sender, Reply to, and trigger for the Campaign under the “Settings” tab

Launch Campaign in SmartRecruiters

1- Launch Campaign              

Once your Campaign is created/updated with all required information, as a CRM Admin, you can click on the Live/Update button to publish or activate the campaign.

Monitor Campaign Performance in SmartRecruiters

Campaign analytics help measure engagement.

  1. As a CRM admin or Talent Community owner, you can click on the Campaign name to see the dashboards of the Campaign.

The system will also show you Email Sent, Open Rate, and Click Rate next to Campaign Name.

Here is the "Email Preview" received by the Candidate.

Conclusion

Campaigns in SmartRecruiters Talent Communities play a crucial role in modern recruitment strategies. By enabling targeted communication with talent pools, campaigns help organizations build stronger relationships with candidates, improve employer branding, and maintain a proactive recruitment pipeline.

With features such as candidate segmentation, email templates, automated communication, and campaign analytics, SmartRecruiters empowers recruitment teams to implement effective recruitment marketing strategies and significantly improve hiring outcomes.

Organizations that effectively leverage campaigns can transform their recruitment process from reactive hiring to continuous talent engagement.

entry.title: Real-Time Architecture in SAP CAP: Mastering Asynchronous Jobs, Gateways with WebSockets
entry.content:
In our previous article (SAP CAP on HANA XSA), we explored how the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) on SAP HANA 2.0 XSA provides the raw power needed for high-performance industrial applications, specifically focusing on mass data ingestion via native Stored Procedures.

However, processing high volumes of data in real-tme is only half the battle. In a mission-critical Industrial Data Application, the user interface must stay in sync with these background processes. If a mass import takes several minutes, a standard synchronous request will lead to Gateway Timeouts and a frustrated user.

This second part of our series focuses on how to close this gap by combining CAP, the SAP Job Scheduler, and Express WebSockets into a responsive real-time architecture.

While the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) is most commonly associated with the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), it offers immense productivity on-premise as well. However, the challenge lies in harmonizing long-running backend jobs (e.g., mass data imports via Stored Procedures) with the need for real-time updates in the frontend (Angular UI) under XSA security and routing constraints (Approuter). Relying on purely synchronous requests in this context risks gateway timeouts and a poor user experience.

In this post, I present a responsive real-time architecture that closes these gaps by combining CAP, SAP Job Scheduler, Angular WebSockets, and native HANA Stored Procedures.

Architecture at a Glance

Our high-level architecture diagram divides the Industrial Data Platform into logical layers. The central data flow is categorized into color-coded zones representing the Client, Gateway, Service, Data, and External Systems.

Core Components of the Architecture:

  • Client Layer (Purple): An Angular UI providing real-time charting dashboards. It communicates with the backend via HTTPS OData APIs and WSS (WebSockets) Event subscriptions.

  • Gateway Layer (Green): The SAP Approuter serves as the central entry point and gateway. It manages API destinations, handles JWT tokens for authentication (linked to the XSUAA/UAA provider), and routes both HTTPS (OData/Actions) and WebSocket traffic.

  • Service Layer (Blue): An SAP CAP Node.js backend extended with express-ws. It contains the standard OData CRUD layer, business logic, and specialized mass data handlers. Central to this is the Express WebSocket Endpoint with a client registry to manage push notifications. This layer is also integrated with the SAP Job Scheduler.

  • Data Layer (Orange): The SAP HANA 2.0 XSA on-premise platform. An HDI Container stores the primary application data in the Column Store. Native HANA Stored Procedures (SQLScript) are used to execute the actual mass data calls and imports.

  • External Systems (Amber): External industrial APIs, historians, or sensor interfaces that act as the source for massive data streams. Native HANA procedures communicate with these systems for data ingestion.

Technical Implementation: WebSocket Registry and Broadcasting

To transmit real-time events efficiently to the Angular UI, we use express-ws within the CAP Node.js backend. This requires a structured registry to manage WebSocket clients based on their specific interests (event types or channels).

In our project, we defined the WebSocket channels as follows. This registry utilizes Sets to store unique WebSocket connections for each business event type:

// db/src/ws/wsRegistry.js (Example location)
'use strict';

// Business-specific registration of WebSocket channels
const wsClients = {
    'validation-status': new Set(), // Critical for the numbered workflow
    'import-status': new Set(),
    'committing-status': new Set(),
    'industrial-trade': new Set(),
    'systemStatusChanged': new Set(),
    'UserChanged': new Set(),
    'process_step': new Set(),
    'job_scheduler': new Set(),
    'program-input-mode': new Set(),
    'batch-job-status': new Set(),
};

module.exports = { wsClients };

The Asynchronous Job Workflow

The most critical part of this architecture is the asynchronous job flow, numbered 1–6 in the diagram. This flow is a best practice for industrial scenarios where jobs cannot be completed synchronously. It demonstrates how CAP and WebSockets prevent gateway timeouts.

Let’s break down the steps:

  1. Angular UI triggers CAP Action (Client -> Service): The process starts when the Angular UI triggers a CAP Action (or service call) via HTTPS. This follows the standard HTTP OData/Actions path.

  2. Fast HTTP 202 Response (Service -> Client): This is a decisive step for UX and timeout prevention. Instead of waiting for the job to finish, the CAP service responds immediately with an HTTP 202 (Accepted). This signals to the frontend that the job has been queued and is running in the background.

  3. CAP Logic calls Stored Procedure (Service -> Data): The CAP backend hands the heavy lifting off to the native HANA layer. This is done via a CALL proc (SQL/ODBC) that triggers the native HANA Stored Procedure (SQLScript).

  4. Native HANA Job Execution (Data -> External -> Data): The Stored Procedure executes the native job. It connects to external industrial APIs, fetches measurement data, processes it, and saves it into the HDI container’s Column Store. This can take seconds or minutes without blocking the UI.

  5. Job Completion & Event Push (Data -> Service) -> WebSocket Trigger: Once the HANA procedure finishes, it sends a status trigger back to the CAP service. The CAP handler then triggers the WebSocket event.

Below is the code snippet showing how the business logic triggers the event using the wsClients registry. In this example, we send a status update to the validation-status channel:

// srv/handlers/validationHandler.js (Example location)
const { wsClients } = require('../ws/wsRegistry'); // Import the registry

// ... Inside business logic, after successful job completion ...
const jobId = result.jobId; 
const final = true; 
const isValid = true; 

// Trigger wsSend with business payload
wsSend("validation-status", {
    jobId,
    status: final ? "SUCCESS" : "IN_PROGRESS",
    request_Id: result?.CheckRequest || result?.Request || result?.request || null,
    isValid: final ? isValid : null,
    detailStatus: final ? 200 : 202,
    resultStatus: final
      ? (isValid ? 'VALIDATED' : 'NOT_VALIDATED')
      : 'PENDING'
});

6.  'JobCompleted' Event Sent (Service -> Client): The Express WebSocket endpoint receives the trigger and uses the registry (wsClients['feasibility-status']) to actively push the JobCompleted event to the connected Angular UI via the WSS path.

Here is the core wsSend broadcasting method. Note how it cleans up closed connections automatically:

// srv/ws/wsBroadcaster.js
const { wsClients } = require('./wsRegistry');

async function wsSend(type, payload) {
  try {
    const clients = wsClients[type];
    const idx = Number(process.env.CF_INSTANCE_INDEX || process.env.INSTANCE_INDEX || 0);
    console.log(`[wsSend] broadcast on instance ${idx}, channel ${type}`);

    if (!clients || clients.size === 0) return false;

    const msg = JSON.stringify({
      event: 'statusEvent',
      data: { type, ...payload }
    });

    for (const ws of clients) {
      try {
        if (ws.readyState === ws.OPEN) {
          ws.send(msg); 
        } else {
          clients.delete(ws); // Cleanup
        }
      } catch (err) {
        clients.delete(ws);
      }
    }
    return true;
  } catch (err) {
    return false;
  }
}

The UI Reaction (Ensuring Consistency)

In response to the incoming JobCompleted event, the Angular UI performs an OData Refresh. This ensures the most recent data is pulled from the CAP service via the standard OData CRUD path, keeping the UI perfectly in sync.

Technical Notes: package.json Setup

To enable this in your CAP Node.js backend, you'll need express-ws. This allows you to integrate WebSockets directly into the Express app that CAP sits on:

{
  "dependencies": {
    "express-ws": "5.0.2",
    "express": "^4"
  }
}

Conclusion

By combining the productivity of SAP CAP with the raw power of HANA Stored Procedures and the push capabilities of WebSockets, we’ve built a resilient real-time architecture for on-premise industrial applications. This asynchronous workflow with HTTP 202 effectively kills gateway timeouts and ensures a fluid user experience.

Key Benefits:

  • Peak Performance: Native HANA procedures handle massive data imports without Node.js overhead.

  • Enhanced UX: Immediate 202 responses and real-time status updates prevent UI lockups.

  • Maintainability: A structured WebSocket registry allows for a clean separation of business channels.

  • Data Sovereignty: The entire stack remains securely on your on-premise HANA XSA infrastructure.

How do you handle mass data jobs and real-time requirements in your CAP apps? Are you already utilizing asynchronous patterns? I’d love to hear your thoughts in the comments!

entry.title: ALE/IDOCs - MATMAS with two BASIC TYPES in one system - Is it possible?
entry.content:
Current State:'

MATMAS message type with Z extension - Basic Type MATMAS01, extension ZMATMAS1.  Interfaces with existing plant location.  Uses enhancement ZXMGVU03.  This has been in place for several years.  We want to keep this in place for now as there is a "tight" time frame to complete the To-Be state below..  We migrated from ECC to SAP RISE in 2024.

To Be State:

Keep existing plant configuration as-is in current state (see above).  For new plant, use MATMAS message type with Z extension, Basic Type MATMAS05, extension ZMATMAS5.  (Extension uses different custom fields than current state extension).   Use Implementation of BADI_MATMAS_ALE_CR.  This is the recommended, MODERN approach (other than using APIs).  Interface with new plant location using Integration Suite.  

I have tried:

1.  Segregating the two states by Basic Type, but current state enhancement does not make the basic type available for checking (in previous versions it was, apparently, but with SAP Rise/S4 that input parameter is no longer available).

2.  Checked change pointers in BDCP2 before executing enhancement in current state and implementation in to-be state.  This did not work....continually received errors.

Any input on how to do this (without having to create a new message type) would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Not able to check "Used for Billing" checkbox while c...
entry.content:
Dear @MananDurge,

Used for Billing is not a manually maintained milestone setting. SAP describes the milestone behavior in the context of billing plans and when a milestone that is used in a billing plan receives an Actual Finish Date the system automatically removes the billing block for the relevant billing plan item

Steps to check:

  • first make sure you are on the correct billing WBS element then
  • make sure the sales order item assigned to that WBS uses a supported setup for this scenario for standard sales orders, SAP lists supported item categories including CBAO (milestone billing plan)
  • finally create the billing plan dates with reference to the project milestones from the sales-order side

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/2ca79d7988b94c6394e700c004393694.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/e7f14402cf5846b4b3d0d677c15414b1.html

If the field is currently No and greyed out in the project milestone that is consistent with standard behavior and usually means the milestone has not yet been picked up by the sales-order billing plan. Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: BAPI <> API
entry.content:
Personal opinion :
S/4 will perfectly accept / consume a BAPI as a (SOAP) Webservice, it is just not the "preferred" architecture from the perspective of S/4 and Clean Core. Yet, it can be an acceptable "intermediate" step along the journey to modernizing your SAP-ecosystem. As far as I know, Mainstream Maintenance on ECC will end by late 2027, so you should definitely take that into account!

Below link shows a scenario where a BAPI (from a Proc.Orches.System) is defined as a SOAP-Service and used as an Artifact within an Integration Suite IFlow. Furthermore, this approach is actually stated as a valid "Modernization Recommendation".

Blog-Link : Modernizing RFC/BAPI-based Integrations for a Clea... - SAP Community
SAP-Help : Modernization Recommendations | SAP Help Portal

You could envisage all kinds of complex 'conversions' of a BAPI-interface to an OData-API etc. but that feels like introducing a lot of complexity. Consider the cost / timeline of that kind of development along with the lifetime of the ECC (how long will that system be around?) ...
Using an IFlow seems like a solid interface-technology and enables you to replace a "SOAP-brick" with an "API-brick" in due time whilst keeping your general connectivity, monitoring, ...

Hope this helps

Nic T.

entry.title: Re: Group Reporting Manual JE Not Picked in 3A Investment...
entry.content:
Hi Naveen,

The translation of investments FS items is special (historical conversion) and the logic depends on the currency translation method that you have assigned to the consolidation unit. For example, if you use the method S0903, the value change of the period is translated using the average rate. But if you select the method Y0902 the value in group currency should be reported, and no translation hapens for the investment FS item.

I suggest you check the currency translation method you use, and that you have posted the value in local and in group currency.

Hope this helps.

Best regards,

Grégoire

entry.title: Understanding Work Schedule in SuccessFactors
entry.content:
I’ll admit it feels a little strange to say this after delivering lots of SuccessFactors projects, but most of my experience has been in EC–WFS–ECP landscapes. In that setup, my involvement with work schedules was usually limited to maintaining a few generic ones in EC while the real complexity lived in the WFS time system, safely handled by my talented colleagues.

But recently, I had to do some Time Tracking work (a module I find fascinating). I didn’t find a lot of documentation on Work Schedule or less than I would have imagined. Naturally, this sparked a desire to dig deeper into the configuration itself. As always when I dig in for something new, I need to understand every detail and every parameter available, how it works and for what business purpose I would use them. So, in this blog, I’m sharing what I’ve learned from my research, with a lot of screenshots to illustrate how the Work Schedule config impacts the time pieces. My goal is to make it easier for you to grasp it all, so you can concentrate on creating cool solutions to complicated requirements.

If you find this useful, don’t forget to hit that little Kudo button at the top. It pays absolutely nothing… but it feeds my ego, which is almost as good.

 

Clock versus Duration: Understanding the Time Recording Variant first!

Before you can choose the right Work Schedule setup, you need to understand the Time Recording Variant. This setting determines how working time is represented in SuccessFactors.

A Time Recording Variant can be configured as:

  • Duration: a total number of hours for a day (8 hours) or
  • Clock: a detailed schedule with start and end times (08h00 AM to 04h00 PM for 8 hours)

 

So… 8 hours versus 8 hours… Isn’t it the same?

Short answer No! It is very different

  • The Duration recording method tells us only how many hours someone works or is absent from work. Time is expressed simply as a total number of hours for the day, without linking those hours to specific times of day. It answers the question “how many hours are worked?” and is ideal for straightforward or flexible work patterns where start and end times aren’t needed.
  • The Clock recording method tells us when someone works or is absent. Time is captured using specific start and end times, allowing you to outline the full structure of a workday, including shifts, breaks, and overnight segments. It answers the question “when exactly does the work happen?”.

 

And why does it matter?

It all comes back to the company’s requirements:

  1. Do we need to know when employees start and finish work or an absence (if it isn’t a full day absence)?
  2. If someone arrives late or leaves early, do we care and do we need to know it?
  3. Do breaks matter to us? Do we need to track when / how long they lasted?
  4. Do we have night, weekend, or after‑hours premiums that depend on the time of day?
  5. Do we need visibility of how many employees were physically on the floor at a specific time?
  6. Do we have shifts that cross midnight and do these need to be captured accurately?

If you are answering YES, then Clock is probably what is best for your case!

 

Is using Clock just better then?

There is no better. While Clock time can sounds like the safer, more complete option, it comes with complexity that many businesses don’t need, can’t maintain, or simply don’t want and a lot of business simply do not need that level of detail and Duration is then a better fit for them.

 

 

Back to our main topic: Work Schedule

A Work Schedule defines the expected working pattern for an employee and serves as the system’s reference for how a normal workday should look. It outlines which days the employee works, how many hours (if Duration) or the detail of all the start and stop time for the day (if Clock). When attendance time or absences are entered, the system can compares them to the Work Schedule to determine regular time, overtime, deviations, and the correct absence deductions.

When creating a Work Schedule in SuccessFactors, you first need to define what the Work Schedule Model will be between:

  • Simple
  • Period
  • Schedule

 

Simple Work Schedule Model

The Simple Work Schedule Model is the most straightforward configuration type.  It is designed for Duration‑based time recording, where employees’ working times are tracked by total hours rather than specific start and end times.

In this model, administrators manually enter the planned working hours for each day, making it an uncomplicated solution for stable, predictable weekly schedules. This simplicity makes it suitable for environments where work patterns do not vary significantly.

 

Period Work Schedule Model

The Period Work Schedule Model is more advanced and flexible than the Simple model, designed to accommodate both duration‑based and clock‑time‑based time recording. It allows organizations to structure work patterns over multi‑day or multi-week or other periods using Day Models, making it ideal for rotating schedules, mixed shift structures, or environments with differentiated working patterns throughout the cycle.

 

Schedule Work Schedule Model

The Schedule Work Schedule Model is designed to reuse an existing Period Work Schedule and repeat its pattern starting from a different Start Date, allowing you to apply the same recurring work pattern across work schedule with less configuration. Instead of rebuilding the same Period Work Schedule structure for every start date scenario, the Schedule Model simply assigns the pattern based on a new reference start date, making it a powerful tool for organizations that need repeating or rotating schedules with flexible start points.

 

Day Model

A Day Model is a standalone object that defines the planned working time for a specific day. It specifies how many hours an employee should work, including segments, start and end times, and breaks. A Period Work Schedule is created by combining multiple Day Models (most of the time… it can be more than just Day Models but let’s keep it simple here). A Day Model can also be reused across different schedules.

When creating a day model, you will need to specify the segment categories between the following (note that I will use Clock Variant in all scenarios):

 

Schedule Working Time:

This is the main segment of a working day. It defines the planned start and end time of the day model in the Clock Variant or the hours expected in the Duration Variant. Only one Scheduled Working Time segment is allowed per day model and it is not possible to create a Schedule working time segment on non-workdays.

When a Day Model is configured only as one Schedule Working Time segment, here it what it looks like on the Timesheet and in the Create Absence Request form:

 

Schedule Unpaid Break:

This segment defines a fixed break during the working day that is deducted from working time. This is about deducting unpaid time from the schedule working time segment.

When a Day Model contains Scheduled Unpaid Break segment, here is what it looks like on the Timesheet and in the Create Absence Request form:

 

Flextime Bandwidth:

The flextime bandwidth extends the working window beyond the scheduled working time so employees can start earlier or finish later within allowed limits. It could also be used to ignore hours done outside that specify window from the timesheet valuation.

When a Day Model contains Flextime Bandwidth segment, here is what it looks like on the Timesheet and in the Create Absence Request form:

 

Night Shift Bandwidth:

This segment is used for cross‑midnight, where an employee starts work late in the evening and finishes the next day. This is really just a Flextime Bandwidth for a Cross-Midnight Shift.

When a Day Model contains Night Shift Bandwidth segment, here is what it looks like on the Timesheet and in the Create Absence Request form:

 

Day Model: Shift Classification

A Day Model Shift Classification is used to categorize a Day Model (or an entire Work Schedule). This classification can later be leveraged in Timesheet Time Valuation, where it becomes possible to extract (or, more precisely, filter) planned time that carry that classification.

For example, if a company pays a $2/hour premium for employees assigned to the Night Shift, adding a shift classification gives us a clear indicator that the employee is scheduled on a Day Model eligible for that premium.

In my view, the name “Shift Classification” is a bit misleading, because the field can be used for far more than shift‑related scenarios. Fully exploring this would require diving deeper into Timesheet Valuation logic… but here’s one way it can be used creatively.

In one of my projects, employees worked on a 28‑day rotation and became eligible for permanency only after completing a certain number of full cycles. To track this, I assigned a shift classification called “End of Rotation” to the Day Model used on the last day of the Work Schedule. In Time Valuation, it then became possible to count how many rotations an employee completed simply by filtering entries where the Day Model had the “End of Rotation” classification via Filter Input Groups (Filter of Shift Classification).

This illustrates how Shift Classification can be used creatively in Valuating time in Time sheet, not just to label shifts and report on it, but to support business logic tied to specific days within a work pattern.

 

Day Model: Rounding Rule

A Day Model can include a grace period rounding rule. This rule helps round out small differences between the scheduled start or end time and the employee’s actual clock‑in or clock‑out time. Instead of counting a few minutes early or late as extra work or a violation, the recorded time is automatically rounded to the planned start or end time. For example, if a 10‑minute grace period is defined, any punch that occurs within 10 minutes before or after the scheduled start time is rounded back to the official start time. This Rounding is created within its own object and then assigned to the Day Model in the Rounding field. Once selected, the system applies the defined rounding logic consistently to all time recordings linked to that Day Model.

 

Day Model: Day Model Usage Types

I have decided that I will create a blog dedicated to Day Model Day Variant soon to make this post a bit lighter and removing the need to talk about Holiday Calendar in this one. Therefore, here I will just explain briefly the concept and add the link to that promised post when it is completed 😉!

A Day Model also needs to define the day model usage type meaning if the day is a normal (or regular) working day or if it is a day variant that will be used define specific reduction of planned working time when a working day is on a Holiday.

Two Day Model Usage Types

  1. Regular Day Model (for Work Schedule assignment): Used for standard working days or non-working days. You define the normal planned hours—for example, 8 hours, or 08:00 to 17:00 with a 12:00 to 13:00 lunch break. Regular Day Model can be assigned as a day Period Work Schedule configuration.
  2. Variant for Partial Holidays (for Day Model assignment): This type of day is not directly assigned to a work schedule, and it applies only in special situations such as half‑day holidays, shortened days before a holiday, or any custom reduced‑hours scenario because of Holiday. A Variant for Partial Holiday Day Model can be assigned as a Variant day in a Regular Day Model configuration.

In summary, a Regular Day Model defines standard daily working hours, while a Variant Day Model defines reduced‑hour versions to be used when the regular day model is on a holiday.

Here is what happens on the Work Schedule when on a Partial Holiday when the Day Variant is configured:

 

Other things to know about Work Schedules in SuccessFactors…

We’ve covered the main configuration elements of Work Schedules and how they (or they could) influence Time Sheet, Time Valuation, and Time‑Off in SuccessFactors. Before closing the topic, here are additional features that are useful and should not be overlooked.

 

Work Schedule Searchable Field

If your company is beautifully complex 😉 and happens to operate with a huge number of Work Schedules, using the searchable field option in the Work Schedule configuration could make your life a bit easier.

With this option, it is possible to search for the right Work Schedule with additional key words when assigning it to an employee, instead of scrolling endlessly through a long list or having to know the exact code. It’s a small configuration choice that can significantly improve efficiency.

 

Assigning Temporary Work Schedule

Temporary Work Schedules are extremely helpful when an employee needs to follow a different work pattern for a short, defined period, for example, covering a colleague for a couple of weeks, switching to evenings for two weeks, or working reduced hours temporarily. Instead of modifying the employees’ permanent work schedule in Job Information (which would create additional slices to the job history), it is possible to assign a Temporary Work Schedule directly in the Time Management section. You simply set the start and end dates, and the system automatically uses the temporary pattern for that period before returning to the original schedule. This approach keeps Job Information clean, avoids unnecessary data maintenance, and provides full flexibility without additional configuration objects.

 

Assigning Individual Work Schedule

When an employee has a very specific working pattern that doesn’t match any existing Work Schedule, such as a custom arrangement, irregular hours, accommodation, or a short‑term one‑off pattern, it is possible to use the Individual Work Schedule option. This allows to manually define the daily working hours directly on the Time management. Just like temporary schedules, this option prevents the need to repeatedly update the Work Schedule field in Job Information, avoiding time‑slice complexity and ensuring the core employment data remains stable.

 

Conclusion

Time is complex… and that’s what makes it fun!

If this blog helped you learn something new, or simply made Work Schedules make more sense, then mission accomplished. Time Tracking has so much potential, and the more of us who truly understand it, the cooler and more elegant the solutions we can build together.

My hope is that this little guide gives you clarity, inspiration, and a few Aha! moments along the way.

Thanks for reading… and happy scheduling!
Stéphanie

entry.title: Re: Cloud ALM を使用したリアルユーザ監視の方法について
entry.content:
ご担当者様、
お世話になっております。
　
恐れ入りますが、デモテナント環境用として提供されているユーザは、データの表示のみ可能なユーザとなり、監視設定の追加や変更等に関する権限は持っておりません。

リアルユーザ監視(RUM)の設定に関する詳細は、下記のドキュメントよりご確認頂けますのでご参照頂ければと思います。
・https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal/real-user-monitoring/run-details.html
・https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/real-user-monitoring
・3459306 - How to configure SAP Cloud ALM Real User Monitoring alerting and e-mail notifications for HTTP errors?
　
尚、RUMの設定の際にエラー等が発生しましたら、現在ご利用のCloud ALM製品をご選択いただき、コンポーネントSV-CLM-OP-RUMとしてケースを起票してくださいますようお願いいたします。
　
以上、よろしくお願いいたします。
SAPサポート

entry.title: Re: Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition
entry.content:
Hi @triciajohnson,

Thanks for this very informative blog. It’s great to see the broader community getting visibility into this new edition of SAP Integration Suite, and the strong response in the comments is a good sign that the topic is generating a lot of interest.

I have a quick question and hope you might be able to clarify. If a client subscribes to the enhanced edition for the development environment, would the same edition also be required for the test and production environments? Or would it still be possible to transport iFlows from dev to test and prod even if those environments use different editions?

I’m also thinking about how best to position the AI capabilities across environments. For example, using this edition in dev environment could be valuable so the integration team can leverage the AI features for generating iFlows. However, considering the 500k message limit and the additional AI features for APIM, it might feel like an inefficient use of cost in a dev environment where transaction volumes are typically low.

On the other hand, if the enhanced edition is only used in production while non-prod environments run on the standard edition, teams may hesitate to use AI-assisted iFlow generation or script optimization directly in production. 

Curious to hear your thoughts on how SAP envisions customers positioning these AI features across dev, test, and production environments.

Thanks again for sharing the insights.

 

Regards,
Nick
